story:
In eighth grade, I volunteered in a kindergarten class at Hayt Elementary in Chicago’s Rogers Park. Two children there shaped a memory I have never been able to shake.

Sam was a round-cheeked charmer with hazel eyes and a grin that fooled adults. When teachers looked away, he snatched crayons, tugged pigtails, and punched boys, then put on innocence like a costume. Kalie was quiet and careful, a little girl with long blond curls who loved coloring inside the lines. She wore old-fashioned, frilly dresses and seemed to glow when she painted.

The class worked in small groups; Sam and Kalie sat across from each other at the red table. One bright spring day they were coloring animals and cutting them out to glue them in order. Kalie’s work was neat and lovely; Sam’s was a scribble. When she asked for the shared scissors, Sam laughed and said, “Come and get them,” lifting them out of reach. She tried and failed, then sat down, tears welling.

A moment later, he shouted, “Oh, here,” and flung the scissors toward her.

I was not in the room when it happened. By the time I returned, the ambulance had already been called. There was blood everywhere, and Kalie was screaming, one hand over her left eye. Later I heard that the scissors had struck her there. Sam folded his arms and insisted she had tripped and fallen. None of the kids could say for certain what had happened in those few seconds.

In the weeks that followed, his parents took him out of school and sent him to therapy, where, I was told, he charmed the psychologist as easily as he charmed everyone else. Nothing was ever proved.

Kalie underwent surgery and eventually wore a glass eye. She transferred to another school. Some children there were cruel and called her names. She withdrew, poured herself into art, and grew into a gifted painter.

Years later she met a kind man and, before he had said a word, told him that she was sorry for the family he had lost. Tall and gentle, he played the flute from memory and spoke softly of the family he had lost. He dictated stories; she wrote them down. They loved each other deeply. They had no children, but they made a quiet life together until, at forty-eight, he died suddenly of an aneurysm.

Sam was sent to private school. Backed by his father, he began to study hard. He excelled in college, trained as an ophthalmic surgeon, married a good woman, and had two sons and a daughter. He won awards, gave talks, and was well liked by colleagues and patients.

On an unusually cool gray day, his secretary knocked on his office door. When he did not answer, she entered and found him slumped in his chair, a pair of surgical scissors driven into his left eye, his throat cut.

Forensics found no usable fingerprints on the scissors, the desk, or the narrow concealed panel behind a cabinet that opened into a small storage space. There were only smears consistent with gloved handling, the sort that might have come from surgical gloves found in every drawer of the building.

Investigators never found evidence identifying who had done such a thing to so prominent and well-liked a surgeon. The case went cold.

Kalie, I’ve heard, later took a long voyage to Europe to study the great masters. She continues to paint and has a modest following. She keeps going, steady as a hand guiding a brush, turning pain toward beauty.
